entry.title: Re: ABAP RAP - Action is not Updating the UI (unmanaged /...
entry.content:
@wjanes 

First, you need to update the entity by `MODIFY ENTITIES` statement.  After that, re-read the updated entity via `READ ENTITIES` and then pass the output to the result parameter.

Example form Help Portal:

  METHOD set_status_accepted.

    " Modify in local mode: BO-related updates that are not relevant for authorization checks
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF /DMO/I_Travel_M IN LOCAL MODE
           ENTITY travel
              UPDATE FIELDS ( overall_status )
                 WITH VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( %tky      = key-%tky
                                                 overall_status = 'A' ) ). " Accepted

    " Read changed data for action result
    READ ENTITIES OF /DMO/I_Travel_M IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY travel
         ALL FIELDS WITH
         CORRESPONDING #( keys )
       RESULT DATA(travels).

    result = VALUE #( FOR travel IN travels ( %tky      = travel-%tky
                                              %param    = travel ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

entry.title: Re: SAP Download Manager error (Unable to read basket con...
entry.content:
Regarding to your issue, please kindly note that SAP Download Manager does not work with multi-factor authentication (MFA) or SAP Universal ID (UID).

If your S-user is linked to UID, do not enter the UID password in SAP Download Manager. The UID password is the one you use to logon SAP for Me.

Instead, enter your individual S-user ID and Password as mentioned in KBA 3016110. 

If you do not remember the password of your S-user, kindly use below link to reset it. When reset your password, please input the S-user ID instead of email address:

Forgot My Password

Then you will use your download manager normally as before.

You can still use your UID password to logon SAP for Me.

Hello,

CR for VS follows the same time line as Crystal Reports Designer, so the end of life is typically a few years in the future.

CR products supports the current less 2 versions. And the dates are extended each time a new release is shipped.

CR for VS only has full versions available so always keep it up to date, typically 2 releases per year.

To many users, so don't expect CR or CR for VS ever to be discontinued, note CR for VS 32 bit is end of life now, only 64 bit is available in the new SP 40 release since there is no reason to support 32 bit applications anymore...

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to re-generate license key for SAP BusinessObject...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

For BOBJ products, if the current license key quantity needs to be reduced, yes, kindly delete the existing license key, then the quantity 200 will be released back.

Then pls create a new license key with the proper number of quantity (e.g. 100) again.

This is documented in KBA 2446681 - Best practices of creating and updating SAP BusinessObjects license keys and system data - SAP for Me:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

  • If it is required to reduce the quantity in an existing license key; delete the existing license key and then request a new license key for the correct quantity.
  • If it is required to increase the quantity in an existing license key, do one of the following:
    • Delete the existing license key and then request a new license key for the correct quantity
    • Request a license key for the additional quantity required as long as the total licenses per deployment complies with your license agreement

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hope this helps. 

Thanks and regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: How to hide fields /Tabs in SAP PPM?
entry.content:
1.- Right-click and then click on Technical Help.

 

2.- Click on the Component Configuration link

 

3.- Select the RIH_EDIT variant and choose CREATE CUSTOMIZING in the "Other Functions" drop-down menu.

 

4.- In Customizing Mode, you can hide elements. Select the views or subviews you want to hide and check in "Hidden Element" checkbox. E.g. Select the View "Deliverables", check in the "Hidden Element" and finally "Save" the changes.

 

5.- If you access the item you will see that the "Deliveries" tab does not appear.

You can create one customizing transport request and accomodate all the changes in a single TR

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA PUBLIC CLOUD EDITITION - CONTENT UPGRADE
entry.content:
Hi Thao,

Below are some of my observations/opinions in my experience in form of answers (with links)to your questions.

Yes—your understanding is aligned with SAP’s described timeline:   software upgrade and content upgrade are decoupled, and the   content upgrade follows after the software upgrade.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/076da842605b4105b6adfbf325695941.html

Q1)Content Upgrade  is essentially SAP-delivered   business process / business configuration content changes .

However, I would  not  phrase it as  it contains  only  business process TRs because in practice, you should treat these as  SAP-delivered content/configuration transports , and the functional team typically validates business impact while Basis/IT manages the transport steps

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/faq-on-upgrading-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13657921

 Q2) Yes,SAP’s 2602 upgrade preparation blog states clearly:
The 2602 Content becomes available after the D-System is upgraded, and a new transport is created … to deliver the 2602 Content to the T-System  . 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/get-ready-for-2602-upgrade-of-sap-cloud-erp-public-edition/ba-p/14307594

So, for the 2602 wave, it is correct to expect a corresponding  2602 Content delivery via transports. 

Q3) This depends heavily on what you mean by generate TRs and which deployment model you are in.
-   the TR creation that SAP explicitly calls out is tied to the CBC Business Content Upgrade   (content transports created in Dev and moved to T/P).

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/faq-on-upgrading-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13657921

In   classic  ABAP upgrade (e.g., SUM on on-prem/private cloud)
It’s normal to have SPDD/SPAU adjustment processes where transport requests are created/used to store adjustment results (different world than public cloud CBC). https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-discussions/how-to-handle-spdd-and-spau-when-starting-the-upgrade-with-a-non-dev-system/m-p/11933199

Q4) 

In my exp., BC Upgrade transport can contain hotfix content and is automatically deployed to your systems, and if it wasn’t released automatically, you may need to take ownership and release it.

you should ensure the hotfix-related content is consistently promoted per your landscape rules, but the exact mechanics depend on whether SAP auto-schedules vs. you manually release/import in your setup.

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-check-the-content-included-in-the-quot-bc-upgrade-quot-transport-in/qaq-p/13855884

Q5)Remember,
- If it appears in the bi-weekly patch cycle  , it is likely hotfix-related (HFC).

- If it appears specifically after   D-system major upgrade completion   and is used to deliver 2602 Content to T-system, it aligns with release content upgrade transports or say BC Upgrade !

Hope it helps!

If I have answered your queries, please help in marking post-answered.

Thanks & Cheers!

entry.title: Re: Mass change of Cost Center and Profit center validity...
entry.content:
Yes — in SAP S/4 HANA fiori, there are fiori applications that allow you to update validity dates for Cost centres and Profit centers in a single step / mass change without having to update each master individually.

Mass Change – Cost centres

This app allows you to change attributes (including validity dates) of multiple cost centres in one run.

Mass Change – Profit centres (or within Manage Profit Centers depending on your S/4HANA release)

You can adjust the validity dates / other attributes for multiple profit centres

These mass change apps help you close the existing cost and profit centres as of 31.12.2025 by updating the “Validity To” date, and then you can create new ones effective 01.01.2026.

✔ Use the mass change fiori apps to set Validity To = 31.12.2025

✔ Use Migration Cockpit to upload the new cost centres and profit centreswith Validity From = 01.01.2026

 

Regards 

Edalada saiteja

entry.title: SAP BW Production Data Refresh
entry.content:
Hi,

We have a requirement to refresh the SAP BW data from the Production system into the SAP BW QA system. Our goal is to refresh only the data, ensuring that system settings and configurations in the QA system are not overwritten by those from the Production environment.

Could you please advise on the recommended approach or procedure to perform this activity?

Thank you.

BR


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP BW Production Data Refresh
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Thank you for contacting SAP Support.

Regarding to your concern, would you please refer to the question '?23. Are the configuration files also saved during a backup?' in Note 1642148 - FAQ: SAP HANA Database Backup & Recovery ?  
By default, the customer specific configurations which have been modified from the default value are not included in HANA database backup.

======================================================
23. Are the configuration files also saved during a backup?

By default the customer-specific configuration settings (.ini files that have been changed from the default) are not backed up as part of a data backup.

Starting with HANA 2 SPS06 you can Backup and Recovery Customer-Specific Configuration Settings.

Backing Up Customer-Specific Configuration Settings

BACKUP DATA Statement

RECOVER DATA Statement

RECOVER DATABASE Statement

Configuration files that contain customer-specific changes can be also backed up and restored manually.

Configuration files that contain customer-specific changes are located by default in the following directories:

For global configuration settings:

?$DIR_INSTANCE/.. /SYS/global/hdb/custom/config? also alias ?cdcoc can be used

For host-specific configuration settings:

?$SAP_RETRIEVAL_PATH?

Configuration files are only created in these directories if customer-specific changes are made to them after installation. If no customer-specific changes have been made, these directories may be empty.
======================================================

Also, you can refer to 'Backing Up Customer-Specific Configuration Settings' in HANA Admin guide. 
If you still have concern, you may copy the configuration files(*.ini) and store them in a separate directory as backup.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: IBP-KF Calculation at aggregated Qty to a Specific Pr...

Here's the suggestion - In copy operator, apply filter to source key figure Actuals Qty as MP Active = Y. Model target key figure as Actuals Qty - MP. Aggregation level should be at Mother Pack in copy operator.

entry.title: Automatic Batch Determination in OBD for NLN Item Category – Unexpected System Behavior
entry.content:
STO is created manually in S/4HANA between a 3PL plant and an external plant.
After STO creation, the Outbound Delivery (OBD) is generated and re-routed to the 3PL via BTP.
Every process is executed in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Issue

In the Outbound Delivery, the system is automatically populating the batch.
This is not part of the intended design.

The requirement is that the 3PL should have the flexibility to select the batch on their own and perform PGI, and the selected batch details should then be updated back in S/4HANA.

 

As per standard SAP behavior, automatic batch determination happens only when the “Automatic Batch Determination” indicator is enabled for the OBD item category (in this case, NLN).

 

However, even after turning off the Automatic Batch Determination indicator for item category NLN, the system is still proposing a batch in the OBD.

Additionally, in some cases, the system is proposing an older batch with zero available inventory, which is causing further issues.

  • Why is the system still proposing batches in the OBD even after disabling Automatic Batch Determination for item category NLN?
  • Is there any other configuration or standard behavior that could be driving this batch determination?
  • How can we completely stop batch proposal in OBD so that batch selection is done only by the 3PL during PGI?

Thanks

Hi,

In my SAP Integration Suite trial tenant, the OData API works perfectly in the browser (logged in via SSO) – I get XML/JSON with real MessageProcessingLogs data.

But when calling the same URL from Postman or curl using OAuth Client Credentials (Bearer token from /oauth/token), I always get HTTP 200 OK + HTML redirect page to /oauth/authorize (interactive login).

Details:

  • Tenant: serverName-trial.cfapps.ap21.hana.ondemand.com
  • Endpoint: /api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs?$top=5&$format=json
  • Created Process Integration Runtime instance (api plan) with roles: ["MonitorDataRead"]
  • Token scopes: it!b196.Default uaa.resource it!b196.IntegrationOperationServer.read it!b196.NodeManager.read it!b196.Roles.Read
  • Headers correct: Authorization: Bearer , Accept: application/json

Tried: new instance/key, role collection assignment to client_id, fresh tokens – still the same HTML.

Why does browser SSO work but Bearer token gets redirected? Missing step in trial tenants for MonitorDataRead?

Thanks for any help!

Sri

SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: Re: process code for routing idoc
entry.content: Hi Dominik — I’ve been running into similar issues with distributing routings and BOMs via ALE, especially when it involves alternative BOMs, routings with multiple counters, alternative sequences/branches, BOM allocations, and object dependencies. So far I’ve tried both /SAPMP/ROUT and POIM, using different message types. In both cases, I’m seeing gaps in the message structures that cause the inbound IDoc to fail or behave inconsistently in the receiving system. For example: • When I use /SAPMP/ROUT, the inbound IDoc fails because the routing alternative sequence/branch number isn’t populated in the IDoc. • When I use POIM, the inbound IDoc see does carry the branch number, but then it references the BOM group number from the sending (DEV) system, instead of resolving/using the group number in the receiving (QAS) system. I was expecting it to derive or map the group number based on what exists inbound. I also tried /LOI/ROU, but I’m not able to set the correct process type (e.g., via BAdI), and I end up with the same error that Mahil mentioned. All that to say: this has been a pretty frustrating rabbit hole, but your post honestly gave me hope that there’s a clean way to troubleshoot this. Question: In your screenshot, what is the transaction code you’re referencing? And how would you use it to analyze issues like the ones above? Also, from your experience, what’s the best way to isolate whether the root cause is: • missing/unsupported fields in the IDoc/message type, • a process/config issue in ALE/IDoc inbound, • or master data assumptions (group counters, internal numbering, allocations, etc.) that don’t translate cleanly across systems? Any guidance on how you’d approach troubleshooting — or what else I should try — would be hugely appreciated. It’s hard to believe there isn’t a stable solution for this.

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 差異の表示
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、ストーリーにて予実差異を表示したい！前年度差異をぱっとわかるようにしたい！といった方向けに、以下のようにチャートやテーブルにおいて差異を表示する方法についてご紹介します。

 目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示
  • チャートでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］
  • テーブルでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成手順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。

※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年2月に作成しています。）

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示は大きく2種類：チャートでの差異表示とテーブルでの差異計算列の追加による表示があります。

チャートでの差異表示では、複数のバージョン間での差異、または複数の期間における差異を値またはパーセントで表示することができます。差異の表現方法は、チャートによってデータラベルや累積、棒グラフやウォーターフォールなど、見たい形によって使い分けることができます。
　
詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：チャートに対する差異の追加 | SAP Help Portal

テーブルでは、計算列または計算列を追加することで差異含め様々な計算された値を表示することができます。
クライアント計算で追加できる一般的な式は以下になります。

詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：テーブルセルでのクライアント計算の作成 (新しいテーブル構築エクスペリエンス) | SAP Help Portal

チャートでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成で作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。

1. 数値ポイントのチャートに、前年度との差異を追加しよう！

 

 

1.1. 数値ポイントチャートをクリックした上、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

 

1.2. （2025年の第2四半期にフィルタをかけます。）「＋フィルタの追加」をクリックします。

 

1.3. 「Data（メンバー）」をクリックします。

 

1.4. 「2025」を展開して「Q2」を選択し、「OK」をクリックします。（2025年の第2四半期にフィルタがかかりました。）

 

1.5. ビルダパネルの「チャートアドオン」を展開し、「差異」をクリックします。

 

1.6. 「＋バージョン/時間の追加」をクリックします。

 1.7. 「Date」を選択します。（今回は前年度差異のためDateを選択しましたが、予実差異の場合はバージョンを選択します。）

 

 

1.8. TO (B)の時間にて、「前の時間間隔」の「ｖ」をクリックします。

1.9. 「前年度」を選択します。（これにより、2025Q2との比較から2024Q2との比較に変更されました。）

 

1.10. 表示オプションの差異の表示形式を「パーセント」、小数部を「1」に変更し、右下の「処理済み」をクリックします。


1.11. 前年度差異を％で表すチャートが完成しました！

2. 棒チャートに、予算と実績の差異を追加しよう！

 

 2.1.数値ポイントチャートをクリックした上、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

2.2. 色の「＋ディメンション/メジャーの追加」をクリックします。

 

2.3. 「バージョン」を選択します。

2.4. 「＋バージョンの追加」をクリックし、「Budget」を選択します。（チャートに実績と予算の両方が表示されました。）


2.5.  ビルダパネルの「チャートアドオン」を展開し、「差異」をクリックします。


2.6. 「＋バージョン/時間の追加」をクリックします。

2.7. 「バージョン」を選択します。

2.8. 表示オプションの差異の表示形式を「パーセント」に変更し、右下の「処理済み」をクリックします。


2.9. 予実差異を％で確認できるチャートが完成しました！

 

テーブルでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

3. テーブルにパーセント差異の列を追加しよう！

3.1. テーブルをクリックした上で右パネル（ビルたパネル）を開き、列の「＋ディメンションの追加」をクリックします。

3.2. 「バージョン」を選択します。 



3.3. フィルタの「Version」をクリックし、「Budget」にチェックを入れて「OK」をクリックします。

3.4.  テーブル内の「Actual」と「Budget」の2つのセルを同時選択して右クリックし、「クライアント計算の追加」>「パーセント差異」>「繰返」をクリックします。

3.5. 追加された「パーセント差異」のセルをダブルクリックし、名前を「予算比」に変更します。

3.6. 予算比が表示されるようになりました！

 

今回は差異の表示方法の紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。
はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Re: Planned values not appearing for activity in CJ20N
entry.content: The issue occurs because SAP cannot derive costing rates from your setup. Even though you entered planned hours, the system needs a valid cost center + activity type combination with maintained plan/actual rates in KP26 (or KP06) to calculate costs. Without this, CJ20N will show “No costs were calculated for the activity.”

entry.title: Re: Example function module ALE_ROUTING_CREATE
entry.content: Hey Jeansy, I’m running into a similar problem sending routings from our DEV system to QAS using different tcodes and message types. Across the scenarios I’ve tested, there’s always some data not filled in the IDoc structures, which causes the inbound processing to fail — and the missing fields vary depending on which tcode/process I use for ALE routing. I also tried generating and sending an IDoc manually by running ROUTING_CREATE_SAMPLE_FILE (FM) directly. That one fails on the receiving side because the BOM allocation in the routing is referencing the BOM group number from the sending system (DEV) instead of the group number in the receiving system (QAS). I was expecting it to resolve against the inbound system’s group/counter. Have you run into this issue (or anything similar to what I described)? For reference, I’ve mainly been testing with POIM and /SAPMP/ROUT, and I’m seeing different gaps depending on the approach. If you have seen this before — where would you suggest starting to understand why it behaves this way? And what’s your usual process for determining what needs to be adjusted (message type/enhancement, inbound processing, ALE settings, mapping logic, etc.) to make routings + BOM allocations distribute reliably?

entry.title: Re: Career Advice: BRIM vs IS-Utilities for an ABAP Consultant
entry.content: Both IS-U and BRIM target different use-cases. BRIM was created as a solution to handle subscription scenarios like Recurring, Usage based etc. BRIM SOM is kind of similar to CRM. Invoicing & Billing is done in BRIM CI (similar to SD billing but we create multiple BITS aka Billable Items and/or CITS aka consumption items, capture all records in a sub-ledger format (FICA) and push to FI. BRIM CC is the pricing, rating engine. So that was BRIM for you in a nutshell. Go for it.

Nice improvements!

entry.title: Re: Planned delivery cost with variance account
entry.content:
MB5B shows quantity stock, but SAP checks valuation method and condition type setup. Having “enough stock” does not guarantee inventory adjustment — it depends on whether the material is MAP valued and whether the tariff condition is inventory relevant in schema.

Check the below areas

Material Master (MM02 → Accounting View)
Is the material valued at Standard Price (S) or Moving Average Price (V)? If it’s Standard Price, differences will always go to PRD.

Condition Type Setup (M/06)
Ensure tariff condition type is flagged as inventory relevant. Check if it’s included in the calculation schema for inventory valuation.

OBYC Settings
Verify that the condition type is linked to the correct account key (e.g., FRE, ZFR, or similar) and not defaulting to PRD.

Invoice Posting (MIRO)
Compare PO planned tariff vs. invoice tariff. If condition type is not properly mapped, SAP posts difference to PRD.

entry.title: Re: GR date earlier than GI date in PO STO
entry.content: First of all, it is not clear what the error message is and what is the business requirement to try this. Nevertheless, GR before GI is not possible in Public Cloud STO. The error is expected system behavior, not a bug. To resolve, always post GI first, then GR. If dates need alignment, adjust the planned GI date in the STO delivery.

entry.title: Enterprise Architecture at Speed of Innovation in 2026 !
entry.content:
A Comprehensive Guide for Digital Transformation 

Table of Contents

  1. Executive Summary
  2. Introduction: The Imperative for Modern EA
  3. Emerging Business Transformation Technologies
  4. Cloud Capacities and Architecture
  5. Enterprise Grade AI
  6. DevOps and Continuous Delivery
  7. API Architecture and Integration
  8. Clean Core Principles
  9. Customized Extensibility
  10. Sovereign Cloud and Data Residency
  11. Scalability Strategies
  12. Cost Optimization and FinOps
  13. Business Strategy Mapping
  14. Enterprise Architecture Management Framework
  15. Essential Skills and Competencies
  16. Implementation Roadmap
  17. Case Studies and Best Practices
  18. Conclusion and Future Outlook

     1. Executive Summary

In the era of rapid digital disruption, the role of Enterprise Architecture(EA) has fundamentally shifted from a rigid governance function to adynamic enabler of innovation. Traditional, static architectural models are no longer sufficient to support businesses that must pivot instantly in response to market demands. This document outlines the framework for "Enterprise Architecture at Speed of Innovation," a strategic approach that integrates cutting-edge technologies, agile methodologies, and robust governance to drive sustainable business value.

Speed of innovation is now a critical survival metric. Organizations that successfully leverage emerging technologies—such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), cloud-native computing, and IoT—while maintaining a stable, "clean" core, significantly outperform their peers. This guide demonstrates how modern EA bridges the gap between high-level business strategy and technical execution, ensuring that every technological investment directly contributes to organizational objectives.

       2. Introduction: The Imperative for Modern EnterpriseArchitecture

Digital transformation is not a destination but a continuous journey ofadaptation. Enterprises today face the dual challenge of managingcomplex legacy systems while urgently needing to adopt modern digitalcapabilities. The "Speed of Innovation" paradigm requires anarchitectural foundation that is both resilient and flexible.

The Need for Agile, Adaptive Architecture

Traditional EA often suffered from the "ivory tower" syndrome,producing artifacts that were obsolete by the time they were published.Modern EA must be agile and adaptive, utilizing iterative processes thatalign with DevOps and product-centric delivery models. This shiftrequires architects to move from being "gatekeepers" to "guardrails,"facilitating rapid development while ensuring security and compliance.

Balancing Innovation with Governance

Unchecked innovation leads to technical debt and securityvulnerabilities. Conversely, rigid governance stifles creativity and speed.The modern Enterprise Architect must strike a delicate balance,implementing automated governance within CI/CD pipelines andestablishing clear standards for "Clean Core" development, allowingteams to innovate safely at the edge.

  1. Emerging Business Transformation Technologies

To architect for the future, one must understand the tools shaping it.Several key technologies are currently redefining the enterpriselandscape.

Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML): Movingbeyond experimentation, AI is becoming embedded in core businessprocesses, enabling predictive analytics, automation, and personalizedcustomer experiences.

Internet of Things (IoT): IoT connects the physical and digital worlds,generating massive data streams that require real-time processingarchitectures and edge computing capabilities.

Blockchain and Distributed Ledger: These technologies offerdecentralized trust mechanisms, essential for supply chaintransparency, smart contracts, and secure multi-party transactions.

Edge Computing: Processing data closer to the source reduces latencyand bandwidth usage, critical for real-time applications inmanufacturing and autonomous systems.

Quantum Computing Readiness: While still nascent, forward-lookingarchitectures must begin preparing for post-quantum cryptographyand the specific optimization problems quantum computers will solve.

  1. Cloud Capacities and Architecture

Cloud is the substrate upon which modern digital business is built. Asophisticated cloud strategy goes beyond "lift and shift" to embracecloud-native principles.

Multi-Cloud and Hybrid Strategies

Enterprises are increasingly adopting multi-cloud strategies to avoidvendor lock-in and leverage best-of-breed services. Hybrid cloudarchitectures remain essential for industries with strict regulatoryrequirements or legacy dependencies, requiring seamless orchestrationbetween on-premise and public cloud environments.

Cloud-Native Design Patterns

To truly leverage the cloud's elasticity, applications must be designed ascloud-native. This involves:

Containerization: Packaging applications with their dependencies forconsistent deployment (e.g., Docker).

Orchestration: Managing container lifecycles at scale (e.g.,Kubernetes).

Serverless Computing: Abstracting infrastructure managemententirely, allowing developers to focus solely on code (FaaS).

  1. Enterprise Grade AI

Implementing AI at an enterprise scale requires more than just datascientists; it requires a robust architectural framework.

AI Governance and Responsible AI

AI Governance ensures that AI models are transparent, explainable, andfree from bias. Responsible AI principles must be codified into thearchitecture, ensuring ethical use of data and algorithms. This includesrigorous audit trails for model decisions and compliance with regulationslike the EU AI Act.

MLOps and Lifecycle Management

MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) applies DevOps principles to ML.It automates the lifecycle of model development, training, deployment,and monitoring. An effective MLOps architecture prevents "model drift"and ensures that AI assets remain accurate and valuable over time.

  1. DevOps and Continuous Delivery

DevOps is the engine of speed. It bridges the gap between developmentand operations, fostering a culture of collaboration and automation.

CI/CD Pipelines

Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipelinesautomate the build, test, and deployment processes. This reduces the riskof human error and allows for frequent, smaller releases—a corerequirement for innovation speed.

Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) treats infrastructure provisioning(networks, virtual machines, load balancers) as software. By defininginfrastructure in code (e.g., Terraform, Ansible), organizations achieveconsistency, version control, and rapid disaster recovery.

  1. API Architecture and Integration

In a connected enterprise, applications rarely stand alone. APIs are theconnective tissue of the modern digital ecosystem.

API-First Design

API-First design prioritizes the API as a product. The contract (interface)is defined before implementation, allowing frontend and backend teamsto work in parallel. This approach promotes reusability and modularity.

Microservices and Event-Driven Architecture

Breaking monolithic applications into microservices allows independentscaling and deployment of functional components. Coupled with Event-Driven Architecture (EDA), systems can react to business events in real-time, decoupling services and improving responsiveness.

  1. Clean Core Principles

A "Clean Core" strategy is vital for maintaining upgradeability andreducing technical debt, especially in ERP environments (e.g., SAPS/4HANA).

Separation of Concerns: Keeping core business logic distinct fromcustom extensions.

Modularity: Designing systems as independent modules that interactthrough well-defined interfaces.

Maintainability: Prioritizing code readability and standardizingdevelopment practices to ensure long-term viability.

By keeping the core clean, enterprises can consume vendor updates andinnovations immediately without breaking custom modifications.

  1. Customized Extensibility

While the core must remain clean, business-specific requirements mustbe met through customized extensibility.

Side-by-Side Extensions

Instead of modifying the core source code, extensions should be builtside-by-side on a platform-as-a-service (PaaS) environment. Theseextensions interact with the core via public APIs and events. This patternensures that the core system remains standard and upgradeable while thebusiness gains bespoke functionality.

Low-Code/No-Code Integration

Empowering "citizen developers" with low-code/no-code platformsaccelerates innovation for non-critical applications, relieving pressure oncentral IT while maintaining architectural oversight.

  1. Sovereign Cloud and Data Residency

In a geopolitical landscape defined by digital borders, data sovereignty isa paramount architectural concern.

Data Sovereignty and Compliance

Architects must design for data residency, ensuring that data belongingto citizens or regulated industries stays within specific geographicborders. Sovereign cloud solutions provide the benefits of public cloud(elasticity, innovation) while guaranteeing legal and operational controlover data access, meeting stringent regulations like GDPR (Europe) orCCPA (California).

  1. Scalability Strategies

Architectures must handle success. Scalability ensures systems performunder variable loads.

Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out): Adding more instances of a resource(e.g., more web servers). This is the preferred method for cloud-nativeapplications.

Vertical Scaling (Scale Up): Adding more power (CPU, RAM) to anexisting resource.

Elasticity: The ability to automatically scale up or down based ondemand, optimizing both performance and cost.

  1. Cost Optimization and FinOps

The variable cost model of the cloud requires a cultural shift towardsFinOps (Financial Operations).

Cloud Cost Management

FinOps brings financial accountability to the variable spend model ofcloud. Architects must design for cost-efficiency, utilizing features likeauto-scaling to shut down unused resources, utilizing spot instances forfault-tolerant workloads, and leveraging reserved instances forpredictable base loads. TCO analysis must now include the "cost of delay"and the value of agility.

  1. Business Strategy Mapping

Technology has no value if it does not serve the business. BusinessStrategy Mapping connects the dots.

Aligning Technology with Business Goals

Enterprise Architects utilize Business Capability Modeling to map whatthe business does to the IT assets that support it. This highlightsredundancy and gaps. Value Stream Mapping identifies bottlenecks inthe flow of value to the customer, directing architectural improvementswhere they have the highest ROI.

  1. Enterprise Architecture Management Framework

To manage this complexity, a structured framework is required. Whetheradapting TOGAF, Zachman, or a custom agile framework, consistency iskey.

Architecture Governance

Governance bodies, such as the Architecture Review Board (ARB), shouldfocus on enablement rather than restriction. Modern governance utilizes"Governance as Code" to automatically enforce standards (e.g., securitypolicies, tagging strategies) within the deployment pipeline.

  1. Essential Skills and Competencies for EnterpriseArchitects

The modern Enterprise Architect acts as a bridge between the boardroomand the server room.

Technical Competency: Deep understanding of cloud, AI, andintegration patterns.

Business Acumen: Ability to speak the language of finance,marketing, and operations.

Soft Skills: Negotiation, storytelling, and change management arecritical for influencing stakeholders without direct authority.

  1. Implementation Roadmap

Transitioning to an innovation-speed architecture requires a phasedapproach.1.

Assessment: establish a baseline of the current application portfolio(TIME analysis: Tolerate, Invest, Migrate, Eliminate).

Target Definition: Define the "North Star" architecture based onbusiness strategy.

Gap Analysis: Identify the steps needed to reach the target.

Execution: Implement via iterative waves, prioritizing "Quick Wins" todemonstrate value early.

     17. Case Studies and Best Practices

Financial Services: The Open Banking Revolution

A global bank transitioned from a monolith to a microservicesarchitecture to comply with Open Banking regulations. By adopting anAPI-first strategy, they not only met compliance but launched a "Bankingas a Service" platform, creating a new revenue stream.

Manufacturing: IoT and Edge

An automotive manufacturer deployed edge computing nodes on thefactory floor. By processing visual inspection data locally using AImodels, they reduced defect rates by 40% and eliminated the latency ofsending high-res video to the cloud.

Common Pitfall: "Resume Driven Development"—choosing technologiesbecause they are trendy rather than because they solve a businessproblem.

  18. Conclusion and Future Outlook

Enterprise Architecture at the speed of innovation is not aboutabandoning discipline; it is about automating discipline to allow forspeed. The future of EA lies in its ability to be the operating system of thedigital enterprise—orchestrating AI, cloud, and human creativity todeliver continuous value. As technologies like Quantum Computing andGenerative AI mature, the EA function will become even more critical ininterpreting these shifts and translating them into competitiveadvantage. Architects must remain perpetual learners, guiding their organizations through the turbulent but rewarding waters of digital evolution.

entry.title: How to Calculate Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) in SAP Sustainability Control Tower – End-to-End
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

This is my first blog post on SAP Community, and I’m excited to share my hands-on experience with the Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) feature in SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

In this blog, I walk through an end-to-end, practical guide on how organizations can capture, calculate, review, and analyze greenhouse gas emissions using CCF. The focus is on explaining the functional flow, key configuration steps, and how emission results are generated and analyzed within the system.

I hope this guide helps anyone who is getting started with CCF or looking for a clear, structured walkthrough of the feature. If you have any questions or suggestions, please feel free to leave a comment below.

Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF)

The Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) process in SAP Sustainability Control Tower helps organizations calculate their greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions based on business activities across different locations. It uses form-based manual data entry and emission factors to compute and report emissions.

Steps involved in CCF Calculation E2E:

  1. Update master data
  2. Maintain emission factors
  3. Create business activities
  4. Create GHG data collections
  5. Enter data in forms in the collection apps
  6. Review data and send to analytics

 

Update Master Data

Import data into the Legal Entity and Business Location dimensions in the Manage Master Data tile.

When exporting the Business Location template, maintain the ‘Legal Entity’ field to establish a relationship link between the legal entity and the business location.

This relationship is required for the CCF feature.

For further details, refer to:

Update Master Data | SAP Help Portal: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUS_SCT/8aa9148b4141433fa2808f9808b65480/66fd41bbe3a646998146f7ed11f4496d.html?locale=en-US#procedure

 

Maintain Emission Factors

Maintaining emission factors is optional but recommended for accurate carbon footprint calculations. Emission factors can be uploaded via file or entered manually and are used to calculate emissions for business activities.

The Manage Emission Factors app provides default data packages from the GHG Emission Factors Hub (U.S. EPA, DEFRA, GLEC, UBA, EMBER), including:

  1. EPA-2023
  2. EPA-2023-Spend
  3. EPA-2024
  4. UBA – 2022
  5. EMBER_2023
  6. DEFER – 2024

If no custom datasets are available, the default packages can be used. However, they primarily cover Scope 1 (Stationary combustion) and Scope 2 (Purchased electricity, steam, and heat) for the United States. Custom emission factor datasets may be required for other locations or scopes.

For further details, refer to:

Maintain Emission Factors | SAP Help Portal: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUS_SCT/8aa9148b4141433fa2808f9808b65480/06ae39b98a3d4789948e81ab8f4bab65.html

 

Create Business Activities

This app is used to define business activities (in any GHG category) for which emissions data will be manually collected and calculated. Activities are linked to specific locations and associated with emission factors. Users later enter consumption data (for example, fuel used or distance travelled), and the system calculates emissions based on the maintained setup.

Separate activities must be created for each location and activity type.

For example, to track emissions for air travel across your organization, a separate Air Travel business activity must be created for each location.

Let’s see an E2E example with screenshots.

  1. Open the Manage Business Activities tile to create a new activity for calculating emissions at a specific location.


  2. Click the Create button to create a new business activity
  3. Enter the required details. In this example, a new business activity for Air Travel is created under GHG Category Scope 3.6 – Business Travel. The GHG Category field offers a list of available categories along with their supported calculation methods for reference.
    Calculation Methods by GHG Category | SAP Help Portal: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUS_SCT/8aa9148b4141433fa2808f9808b65480/e6790f0245674b89a27397b5d4ae26eb.html


  4. Emission factors can change over time. To reflect these changes, Emission Factor Periods are maintained within the Manage Business Activities app.

    Each period stores updated emission factors and calculation details for a business activity. During footprint calculation, the system uses the emission factor valid for the relevant period, ensuring accurate and time-specific results. Create Emission Factor Periods by clicking on the Create button.
  5. When a new emission factor period is created, it defaults to the activity’s end date. For the next emission factor period, enter the new start date — the system automatically updates the previous emission factor period’s end date.
  6. Under Footprint Details, select the Preferred Footprint Type to define the kind of data users will enter in the Record GHG Data tile.

There are three types:

  1. Manual Emission Footprint
  2. Quantity-Based
  3. Spend-Based

For GHG Category Scope 3.6 – Business Travel, supported calculation methods are Fuel-Based, Distance-Based, and Spend-Based.

  1. Quantity-Based and Spend-Based footprints calculate total CO₂e using emission factor data.
  2. For Manual Emission Footprints, users enter the total CO₂e directly.

In this example, select Quantity-Based footprint type with Fuel-Based calculation method, choosing Manual Entry as the Data Source.

(This is because default emission factor packages are currently only available for Scope 1 and Scope 2.)

After selecting Manual Entry, enter the emission factor:

  1. 2.68 kg CO₂e per liter of fuel.

Note:

  1. The entered value represents the total CO₂e produced per quantity.
  2. The system uses this to automatically calculate emissions based on the quantities that users record in the Manage GHG Data Collections and Record GHG Data apps.
  3. To simplify maintenance, you can use a mass upload feature to import multiple business activity records at once.

For further details, refer to: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUS_SCT/21ecfb83f8d14e5982b7af315da5889a/f8f943508363488ba184965cf4442d46.html

Click Apply to save the details.

Then, click Create button.

 

Create GHG data collections

Use this app to create a periodic data collection form to capture business activity data for each location within a given period.

The Manage GHG Data Collection app helps in recording footprint data linked to business activities. You create a data collection for the relevant period, and users can enter data through GHG data forms, accessible from both this app and the Record GHG Data app

Let’s see an E2E example with screenshots.

  1. After setting up the business activity with the necessary Emission Factor Periods, open the Manage GHG Data Collection app.
  2. Click the Create button to create a GHG Data Collection for the relevant period.  
  3. Then select the Footprint Inventory Scope is “Default (DEFAULT)” and the relevant period. 

     

  4. Then click Create button.

     

  5. After creating the new data collection, it appears in the table with the status "In Progress" and 0% Data Recording Completion. Select the created GHG Data Collection to capture business activity data.
  6. After selecting data collection item in the tables, the GHG Data Collection screen displays general information along with the GHG Data Forms table, which shows all in-scope locations and their data collection progress.

 

Enter data in forms in the collection apps

  1. After selecting a specific location (e.g., Chennai (53002)), you’ll be redirected to the Record GHG Data Form screen, where you can enter GHG data for the selected period and any business activities maintained for that location. Click the Edit button to record GHG Data.
  2. Enter the quantity of fuel used for the business activity at the selected location, then click the Save button to store the data.
    The value 7.01624 represents the total CO₂e emissions generated from the consumption of 2618 liters of petrol. This result is expressed in metric tons, where 1 ton = 1000 kilograms.

    The system used the emission factor of 2.68 kg CO₂e per liter, so:
    2618 liters × 2.68 kg CO₂e/liter = 7016.24 kg CO₂e,
    which is 7.01624 tons CO₂e after converting kilograms to tons.

    This calculation helps quantify the carbon footprint based on the amount of fuel used.

  3. You can mark entire locations or specific business activities as Not Applicable if no data needs to be collected for that specific period. These settings are remembered for future collections, reducing repetitive maintenance.

     
  4. Once all required values are entered, the Data Recording Completion will show 100%, but the status remains In Progress. To finalize the entry, click Set Completed and then Save. The status will change to Completed, and the data becomes available for footprint calculations.

Note: You can still make edits to a form marked as Set Completed or Set Not Applicable by clicking Reopen.

 

Review data and send to analytics

Step 1: Review and Publish GHG Data

Before GHG data can be analyzed or published in the View Emissions Data app, it must be reviewed and confirmed.

  1. Open the Manage GHG Data Collections app.
  2. Select the relevant reporting period and business locations.
  3. Click Review, enter a comment, and click Confirm.

Once confirmed:

  • The Review Status changes to Reviewed.
  • Data becomes available in the View Emissions Data app.

Note: If any data is modified after review, the status updates to Outdated, and a re-review is required.

If the review fails, try again.

Note:
Use the Regenerate option when changes are made that affect an existing GHG Data Collection. For example, if new business activities are created after a data collection has already been generated, regenerating the collection updates the data forms to include those new activities. Previously entered data is retained and not lost during regeneration

Step 2: Analyze Recorded GHG Data

Use the View Emissions Data app to analyze the collected and confirmed data.
The Emissions Dashboard provides insights into:

  1. Gross GHG Emissions
  2. Net Emissions
  3. Emissions Avoided by Renewables
  4. Offsets

This helps monitor trends and evaluate sustainability performance across locations and periods.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog. I hope this end-to-end walkthrough of the Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) feature in SAP Sustainability Control Tower was helpful and provided clarity on how emissions can be captured, calculated, reviewed, and analyzed in the system.

If you have any questions, suggestions, or would like to share your experiences, please feel free to leave a comment below. I’d be happy to engage and learn from the community.

Stay tuned for upcoming blog posts, where I’ll continue sharing practical insights on SAP Sustainability solutions, new features, and technology-focused topics in SAP PaPM Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Stock Transfer Order Delivery completed indicator
entry.content:
Good day @Pdud_97 

Thank you for your question.

Goal:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, we want an automatic way to close such STO items—e.g., if the remaining quantity has not been delivered within 7 days, automatically set Delivery Completed (ELIKZ) on the STO item so it stops appearing in open order backlogs.

The simple answer is NO.

The reason is, in public cloud, the system only automatically sets Delivery Completed (ELIKZ) when the PO (STO) item is fully received or if you explicitly set the indicator manually. The public cloud does not provide any standard configuration to set ELIKZ automatically based on a time window or aging rule as you desire. This is described in KBA 3620796.

  • The public cloud offers a workaround to set ELIKZ during Goods Receipt. This allows you to set Delivery Completed at item level. However, only if you are not expecting further receipts.

Here is a quick guide:

Open Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document, enter your STO  PO. In the item, set the Delivery Completed indicator (make sure it is the final delivery) and post GR.

  • Alternatively, you can set ELIKZ in the STO manually as mentioned above. Do this by opening Manage Purchase Orders, open your STO, go to the item Delivery data, tick Delivery Completed and save.

open your STO, go to the item Delivery data, tick Delivery Completed and save.

Considering the public cloud does not allow you to configure “automatic after 7 days” an option would be to configure an extension outside the standard logic. Automation/logic can come in handy here because it allows you to periodically evaluate open STO items and update them through a supported interface.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Adding Custom Fields to a Fiori App Using Custom Fields and Logic
entry.content:
Introduction: 

SAP S/4 HANA provides in-app extensibility and the Adaptation Transport Organizer (ATO) framework to safely create, enable and transport custom fields without modifying standard code. In this blog, I will walk through a step-by-step process to set up ATO, prepare custom fields, enable them, publish them and finally add then to a custom Fiori app.

Step 1: Initial ATO (Adaptation Transport Organizer) set up:

  1. Go to transaction SCFD_EUI

2. Close the initial pop up and click on Auto Setup and following screen will populate:

3. Create below packages using SE80:

  1. Local package TEST_KEY_USER_LOCAL
  2. Sandbox package TEST_KEY_USER_SANDBOX

4. Now enter the local and sandbox package name and click on ‘Setup with specific data’.

5. It will then prompt you for a transport, for this first create a workbench transport and assign it in the popup to enter the transport and save your entries.

6. Input table name ‘GMGR’ and field ‘Z*’ and hit Execute:

Step 2 – Understanding Field Statuses

After execution the output screen shows three possible field statuses:

Enabled: This is already enabled and available in custom field APP

Can Be Enabled: This can be enabled by clicking Enable button in tool bar

Preparation Required: Click on Prepare button, it will show the further steps to make it

‘CAN BE ENABLED’ status.

Step3: Changing Status from preparation required to can be enabled

  1. Select ZZPIAR_EMAIL and click on Prepare.

2. The message below will be shown:

3. Go to T-code SE11 and check GMGR table.

4. The field ZZPIAR_EMAIL does not have domain associated

5. Once Domain is created for this field, the status of the field will become ‘CAN BE ENABLED’.

Step4: Enabling the custom field

  1. The below field ZZFINALEXP is in ‘CAN BE ENABLED’ status:

2. Select ZZFINALEXP and click Enable button:

3. Select correct enabling type and click ok:

4. Select the package and create a workbench transport and Provide Transport number at Request/Task click ok.

5. We can see that the field ZZFINALEXP status changed to ‘Enabled’ status:

Step5: Publish the custom Field

  1. Go to T-code /n/ui2/flp
  2. Login to FIORI
  3. Execute ‘custom field’ app
  4. The below screen appears, this app shows all ‘Enabled’ fields, we see ‘Final Expense’ field here which we have enabled in above steps(step3).
  5. Now select ‘FINAL EXPENSE’ field and click on the arrow.

6. The screen below opens, now enable Additional selection-Grant

7. Click on publish

The field is published now.

Step6: Add Custom field to ‘Custom’ App

  1. Now go to the custom app and click on ‘Adapt UI’ as shown below.

2. Now the page becomes ‘Editable’, right click on screen, we can see option add field

3. Select Final expense field here and click OK.

4. Final Expense field will be added to the screen, now click Publish and save and exit.

Conclusion:

By following these steps, you can manage custom fields using SAP’s standard extensibility framework without touching the core objects. This approach reduced upgrade risk, supports transport consistency, and aligns with SAP’s clean-core strategy. The same method can be reused for other tables and fields across S/4HANA landscape.

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Product Updates (HY2-2025) and Road Map Highlights (HY1-2026)
entry.content:
Introduction

Sustainability reporting is shifting from annual disclosure to continuous, operational performance management, and that requires sustainability intelligence that runs inside the business, not beside it. SAP’s 2026 position is straightforward: sustainability AI must run where the business runs, inside your ERP, or it won’t be truly intelligent, scalable, or manageable.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower supports this by grounding sustainability insights in a trusted enterprise context and embedding them into the end‑to‑end lifecycle of scope definition, governed data collection, analysis, and AI‑assisted reporting, to enable faster, lower‑effort compliance and measurable business impact.

With that framing, the updates in HY2 2025 focus on strengthening the end-to-end path from scope → data → insight → disclosure, and our HY1 2026 roadmap continues to expand regulatory coverage, auditability, data quality controls, and AI-enabled reporting so that customers can achieve faster, lower-cost compliance with measurable business impact.

Q3 & Q4 2025 Highlights for SAP Sustainability Control Tower

Content Factory: Accelerating regulatory metric content at scale

To support customers on their ESRS journey, SAP SCT continues to expand SAP‑provided sustainability metric content, covering both quantitative and qualitative ESRS requirements, while reducing duplication and improving mapping quality.

 

We significantly increased ESRS coverage to help customers meet ESRS data-point requirements, with clear visibility into coverage progress. As communicated in our Help Portal, SAP SCT’s ESRS datapoint coverage and the number of released/planned metrics increased substantially through 2025, with the aim to enable ESRS reporting based on a strong data foundation and sourcing capabilities.

Reporting Scope Management: Turning DMA results into actionable data requirements

ESG data collection relies on a clear reporting scope, usually based on a company’s double materiality assessment (DMA), to determine what to report, when, how, and by whom. SAP SCT’s Reporting Scope features help turn disclosure requirements into specific, system-managed data needs.

With Reporting Scope Management, you can:

  • Capture the outcome of materiality assessments (“what to report”) and map it to SAP-provided ESRS metrics or your own custom metrics.
  • Define scope items across organizational structures and reporting cycles so the scope becomes the backbone for workflows, analysis, and reporting.

Ensure traceable collection and reporting over periods to make scope changes transparent and auditable over time.

 

Formula-based metric definition: Configure sustainability KPIs with a flexible Formula Builder

Sustainability reporting rarely ends with out-of-the-box metrics alone. That’s why SCT supports the creation of custom sustainability metrics with the same metadata richness as SAP‑provided metrics, and now with a Formula Builder that makes defining calculation logic far more accessible.

With a formula-based metric definition, you can:

  • Create custom sustainability metrics that are not part of SAP delivery and assign consistent metadata.
  • Define variables mapped to source measures and apply dimension filters (for example, restrict to a specific entity, location, or category).
  • Use the formula builder to define expressions that support arithmetic, conditional, and aggregation logic, helping you align internal KPIs or industry-specific requirements with your reporting model.

Analyze ESG Data app: Personalized insights, drilldowns, and governed access

In HY2 2025, we continued to evolve the analytics experience with the Analyze ESG Data application, a flexible, personalized way to explore both SAP-provided and custom metrics, replacing static KPI tiles with a more dynamic analysis layer.

 Key capabilities include:

  • Drilldown analysis across organizational and metric dimensions (for example, business location, reporting line, legal entity, time period, and predefined metric dimensions).
  • Filtering and grouping by time, hierarchy, sustainability matter, reporting frameworks (like ESRS / EU Taxonomy), or user-defined categories.
  • Personalization: save and share views to align analysis across stakeholders and teams.
  • Governance: instance-based authorization supports secure data access and separation of duties.

Enriched AI-based ESG reporting: Customizable templates + in-app AI actions

SAP SCT’s AI-assisted reporting capabilities continue to mature, focusing on reducing manual effort while increasing control, structure, and reusability. In HY2 2025, we strengthened report generation with:

  • User-defined templates to structure ESG reports and include SAP‑shipped quantitative metrics alongside charts and tables from a pre-shipped list.
  • AI actions for in-app editing, enabling users to refine generated text (e.g., shorten or lengthen it) directly within the report.

This allows sustainability teams to move quickly from data to narrative while maintaining consistent, reviewable reporting results that align with internal disclosure requirements.

Our Initiatives: Linking sustainability ambition to execution

SAP SCT is designed to help organizations move beyond compliance into performance management, including tracking sustainability initiatives with transparency into benefits and costs. Our Initiatives App capabilities focus on turning goals into actionable work with traceability.

With Our Initiatives, organizations can:

  • Create and manage initiatives across lifecycle statuses with detailed metadata (impacted metrics, financials, benefits, actions).
  • Capture CapEx / OpEx, business-as-usual comparisons, and sustainability impact with system-supported calculations and guidance.
  • Break initiatives into actions, assign owners, and track changes to support structured planning and measurable execution.

One API: Unified Sustainability Metrics API for reporting & disclosure consumption

To simplify integration with disclosure tools, reporting platforms, and external consumers, SCT introduced a unified Sustainability Metrics API (One Read API) for metric value consumption.

Key capabilities:

  • Unified retrieval of metric values across quantitative and qualitative metric types.
  • Exposure of metric metadata, including ESG standard references for tagging purposes (important for downstream disclosure).
  • Bulk retrieval based on reporting scope, period, and hierarchy; plus flexibility for time aggregation and hierarchy selection.
  • Ongoing support for current APIs remains, but updates are planned primarily in the unified API going forward.

CPE‑UM value scenarios: extensibility for advanced ESG scenarios

SCT’s value scenarios can be extended with CPE‑UM, which enables read/write access to SCT’s data foundation for advanced customer scenarios.

This helps implement scenarios such as:

  • Sample reporting (read scenario) using a connector architecture for performance and flexibility.
  • Custom metric calculation (read/write) by consuming SCT data and writing back calculated values.
  • Custom data consolidation (write scenario) to operationalize ESG data provisioning into SCT.

 H1 2026 Roadmap Highlights

Forward-looking statement: The following roadmap items are planned and may change.

For HY1 2026, SCT priorities focus on: expanding regulatory coverage, market-ready reporting scope & data collection, enhancing analytics & reporting, and wider adoption & integration options. 

IFRS S1 / S2 coverage (Regulatory expansion & interoperability)

We plan to expand regulatory content beyond ESRS by adopting IFRS S1 and S2 where interoperability exists and by providing preconfigured metric definition content to help customers address investor-oriented baseline disclosure requirements.

Link to Road Map Item

Evidence Management (MVP) for Audit Readiness

To strengthen auditability across the ESG lifecycle, SCT plans an Evidence Management capability (MVP) as a central application to attach, manage, and access documents related to ESG data entries.

Planned core capabilities include:

  • Central repository for attachments linked to reference objects with metadata.
  • Upload/download / metadata updates/deletion, including multiple attachments per object.
  • End-to-end scenario staged across quantitative data and qualitative ESG data, with later integration into data collection forms.

Link to Road Map Item

Data products: Sustainability Metrics (consumption & integration scenarios)

We plan to strengthen “data product”- style consumption and integration scenarios so customers can adopt prebuilt integration paths for key measures (e.g., energy consumption and workforce data), with mapping and low-touch operations.

Link to Road Map Item

DMA process coverage & Reporting Scope enhancements

To operationalize the DMA-to-disclosure pipeline, roadmap items include improvements around reporting scope import/export and interfaces to push DMA lists into reporting scope, plus enhancements in data collection configuration driven by scope.

Link to Road Map Item

Multiple language and format support in AI reporting

Planned enhancements for AI-assisted reporting include:

  • Ability to generate reports in multiple languages.
  • Improved output formats (for example enhanced export options) and more flexible generation options (such as filtering at hierarchy level).

Link to Road Map Item

Custom validation rules & plausibility checks for data import

To improve data quality and reduce the risk of incorrect disclosure, SCT plans configuration options for custom validations and plausibility checks, including rules for value ranges, dimension governance, dependency rules across value combinations, and time-based plausibility checks using historical comparisons and tolerance thresholds.

Link to Road Map Item

Stay tuned for more and check the SCT Roadmap Explorer.

Additional Information

 For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, we recommend browsing the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

entry.title: Re: INVENTORY VALUTION AT COMPANY CODE LEVEL
entry.content:
Hello @Dinesh_158 

Thank you for your question.

Is there a business reason behind that set-up request?

The public cloud does not permit setting inventory valuation level per company code. The reason is:

  • The valuation level is set client wide and not something you can selectively switch on for only one company code.
  • The public cloud inventory is based on plant, and the classic customisation (OX14) is not available for you to change.

So, with 10 company codes, you cannot make only 1 company code while the rest remain plant valuation. See Plant, Company Code, and Valuation Area

I recommend you use plant level valuation and control any valuation differences by:

  • Maintaining different material prices per plant
  • Structure each plant per company code as you need.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to: Open Embedded Url added using Adapt UI in a new Window for SAP Fiori App ?
